text: We present a video of a synchronous laparoscopic left lateral sectionectomy with a laparoscopic right hemicolectomy for a cecal carcinoma presenting with a synchronous liver metastasis.
The case is a seventy-five year old gentleman who presented to our unit with a cecal adenocarcinoma which was detected through the UK's bowel cancer screening programme.
He was found to have a synchronous left lobe metastasis.
A CT scan of his abdomen shows a cecal tumour
indicated here by the red arrow, and the liver metastasis in segment three is best demonstrated on the MR scan, again as indicated here by the red arrow.

The diagram illustrates the port placements for the laparoscopic procedure, showing two twelve millimeter ports and two five millimeter ports positioned to facilitate both liver and colonic resections.
The first stage of a procedure is the left lateral sectionectomy.
The laparoscopic camera provides a view of the liver, allowing the surgeon to identify the metastasis and assess for any signs of extrahepatic spread.
The surgeon mobilizes the left liver by dividing the left triangular ligament using a hook diaphragm, allowing for better access and visualization of the liver structures.
A surgical swab is placed under the liver to protect the adjacent oesophagus and stomach from potential damage during the procedure.
A nylon tape is placed around the left lobe of the liver, providing traction during the transection of the liver parenchyma.
The surgeon divides the left coronary ligament up to the level of the left hepatic vein, completing the mobilization of the left liver.
The surgeon uses intraoperative ultrasound scanning to confirm the position of the metastatic disease in the liver and to rule out other lesions not visible on preoperative scans.
The surgeon uses a hooked diaphragm to mark the transection line on the surface of the liver, preparing for the parenchymal transection.
The surgeon prepares for a left lateral sectionectomy by identifying the level of the falciform ligament on the liver.
The surgeon uses the lotus liver resector to transect the liver parenchyma, following the marked transection line and utilizing nylon tape for traction on the left lobe.
The lotus system is a torsional ultrasonic scalpel which delivers energy perpendicular to its blade.
The surgeon begins the transection at the ligamentum teres, following the premarked line parallel to the falciform ligament on the liver surface.
The surgeon continues the deeper transection just lateral to the falciform and round ligament complex, using the premarked line as a guide.
The surgeon encounters the left portal inflow as the first portal structure during the procedure, visible within the surgical field.
The surgeon uses a laparoscopic endo-GIA stapler to safely divide the identified structure within the surgical field.
The surgeon uses the lotus energy device to continue transecting tissue up into segment two of the liver, approaching the left outflow structures.
The surgeon identifies the left hepatic vein and uses a laparoscopic endo-GIA vascular stapler to safely divide it.
The surgeon uses multiple applications of the laparoscopic Endo-GIA stapler to ensure the left hepatic vein is securely sealed and ligated.
The surgeon uses the Lotus Energy device to safely seal vessels or pedicles that are less than five millimeters in diameter, ensuring hemostasis in the surgical field.
The Pringle maneuver is not usually required for a left lateral sectionectomy.
The surgeon completes the transection at the level of the left triangular ligament, using the Lotus Energy device to safely dissect the ligament.
The surgeon checks the transection plane for haemostasis, observing for any bleeding as the central venous pressure is increased by the anesthetist following the completion of the transection.
The surgeon applies haemostatic adjuncts, floceal and ticeal, to the transection plane to ensure effective bleeding control.
Any bile leakage should also be dealt with at this stage.
The right hemicolectomy is usually performed as the second stage of this procedure.
The surgeon observes the cecum, which appears inflamed and is adhered to both the omentum and the anterior abdominal wall.
The surgeon uses a ligature energy device with a Maryland tip to safely divide the adhesions between the inflamed cecum and the surrounding structures.
The surgeon separates the inflamed cecum from the anterior abdominal wall, carefully detaching it from the surrounding tissue.
The surgeon begins mobilizing the right colon using a medial to lateral approach, carefully separating it from surrounding structures.
The surgeon divides the peritoneum on the medial side of the mesocolon and pushes it downwards, aiming to achieve a total mesocolic excision for optimal oncological outcomes.
The surgeon performs dissection down to the origin of the ileocolic artery, carefully navigating through the surrounding tissue.
The surgeon continues to dissect around the middle colic and right colic arteries, ensuring careful separation from surrounding tissues.
The surgeon identifies and preserves the ureter and gonadal vessels during the dissection, ensuring these structures remain intact.
The surgeon performs an omental resection due to firm adhesion of the omentum to the cecum and the anterior abdominal wall, ensuring complete removal for oncological purposes.
Here we show a laparoscopic dance where one Johan
The surgeon alternates the ligature ligation technique to divide the omentum, moving the instrument in a cyclical manner.
The surgeon divides the omentum up to the level of the transverse colon, then moves the transverse colon cordially.
The surgeon exposes and enters the lesser sac, revealing the anatomical space behind the stomach and adjacent to the pancreas.
The surgeon completes the lateral dissection of the right colon by dividing the lateral peritoneal folds, separating the colon from the surrounding peritoneal tissue.
The surgeon uses a ligature device to achieve further division of the omentum, continuing the alternating ligation technique.
The surgeon completes the mobilization of the right colon, moving it medially in preparation for colonic resection and subsequent anastomosis.
The duodenum and the head of the pancreas are visible, along with the ileocolic pedicle, which has been ligated, divided, and secured with hemoclips.
The surgeon has divided the ileocolic artery, right colic artery, and middle colic artery at their origins from the superior mesenteric artery (SMA).
An endocatch device is inserted through a 12mm port, and the liver specimen is placed inside the device for retrieval.
The surgeon secures the mobilized cecum using a Johan grasper, preparing it for further surgical steps.
A small incision is made as a midline extension of the existing midline twelve millimeter port.
Our patient made a good recovery following his operation and was discharged home following a hospital stay of ten days.
Both for colonic resection and liver resection had margins of R-naught.
We demonstrate in this video that laparoscopic synchronous resection for colorectal cancer and liver metastasis is both feasible and safe.
